_general: Carbonized by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in AD 79 and buried in the Villa of the Papyri at Herculaneum — the only library to survive from classical antiquity. Recovered in excavations begun in 1752; too fragile to open physically, it is read non-invasively via X-ray tomography and virtual unwrapping.

timeline.title: Naples reveal (press conference)
timeline.blurb: Results across Scrolls 1, 4, 5 and PHerc 0139 to be presented at the papyrology conference in Naples.

timeline.title: Scroll 1 ink predictions refreshed
timeline.blurb: New ink-detection passes on the 2.4µm Paris 4 data.

timeline.title: PHerc 0139 title recovered
timeline.blurb: Identified as Philodemus, On Gods, Book 8. ~20% unrolled; 1µm slabs give real legibility gains.

timeline.title: Scroll 4 banner read
timeline.blurb: Full-scroll ink banner read end to end — the first scroll read essentially in its entirety.

timeline.title: Scroll 5 ~70% unrolled
timeline.blurb: A major virtual-unrolling milestone, though ink legibility remained limited at the time.

timeline.title: 2023 Grand Prize
timeline.blurb: First substantial passages of Scroll 1 read — 15+ columns of Greek Epicurean prose.

timeline.title: First Letters
timeline.blurb: First ink letters detected inside an unopened scroll (Scroll 1) — proof the method works.

scrolls.desc:
PHerc. Paris 4 is a rolled Herculaneum papyrus from the [Institut de France](https://www.institutdefrance.fr/en/home/), carbonized during the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. It was first scanned at the [Diamond Light Source](https://www.diamond.ac.uk) in Oxfordshire, UK (2023, at 7.91 µm), and rescanned in 2026 at the [ESRF](https://www.esrf.fr) in Grenoble, France (2.4 µm, plus 45.5 µm overview scans).

It is the scroll with by far the most segments, and the first from which substantial continuous Greek text — Epicurean philosophy attributed to Philodemus — was recovered: the [first letters](https://scrollprize.org/firstletters) were discovered here, and the [2023 Grand Prize](https://scrollprize.org/grandprize) was claimed on it.
scrolls.label: PHerc. Paris 4
scrolls.repository: Institut de France, Paris

content.work: Epicurean prose, attributed to Philodemus (title not yet identified)
content.summary: Scroll 1 — the flagship. Substantial continuous Greek text recovered from the banner and body; site of the 2023 First Letters and Grand Prize.
content.caveat: The title region shows no detectable ink (possibly a different ink; the top rows are physically missing).

readings.status: Substantial continuous Greek text recovered; ink clearly legible at 2.4µm.

images.cap: Ink prediction overlaid on the 3D papyrus surface (2.4µm)

legalNotice.title: Data access and publication notice
legalNotice.licenseLine: These terms are a modification of the CC BY-NC 4.0 license (additional terms) under which these materials are provided. The publication-reservation period runs until 25/06/2027.
paragraphs: The raw scan data, virtually unwrapped segments, ink-enhanced data, ink identifications, and related derived materials for this ancient scroll are being made available to support open examination, study, verification, and technical experimentation. Access to these materials is provided on the express condition that they are not used for scholarly publication during the temporary publication-reservation period.

For one year from the public release of these data, scholarly publication rights are reserved for the research teams designated by the Vesuvius Challenge or by the custodial institution holding the physical scroll. Those teams have been granted the right to prepare the first scholarly analyses because they were designated by the Vesuvius Challenge or by the institution responsible for the original material. This publication reservation was a condition of the imaging of the scroll and must be respected by all users.

During this period, users may examine, study, test, and analyze the data, but may not publish scholarly claims, transcriptions, translations, editions, interpretations, or formal analyses based on the data without permission from the Vesuvius Challenge, the custodial institution, or the designated scholarly teams. After the one-year restriction has expired and the designated teams have published their analyses, the data will be released from this publication restriction and will be available for unrestricted scholarly use.

scrolls.desc:
PHerc. 1667 is a rolled Herculaneum papyrus from the [Biblioteca Nazionale di Napoli](https://www.bnnonline.it/it/121/officina-dei-papiri-ercolanesi), carbonized during the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. It was first scanned at the [Diamond Light Source](https://www.diamond.ac.uk) in Oxfordshire, UK (2023, at 3.24 µm and 7.91 µm), and rescanned at 2.4 µm at the [ESRF](https://www.esrf.fr) in Grenoble, France (2025). From that scan it became the first Herculaneum scroll ever virtually unwrapped and read in full, end to end, without being physically opened.

It is similar in size to PHerc. 332 (Scroll 3).
scrolls.label: PHerc. 1667
scrolls.repository: Biblioteca Nazionale di Napoli ‘Vittorio Emanuele III’ (Officina dei Papiri Ercolanesi)

content.summary: Read end to end — the lower parts of about twenty-two columns across roughly 1.4 m of papyrus, transcribed and reviewed by papyrologists. A philosophical treatise on ethics; the evidence points to a Stoic work, and its final preserved column names Aristocreon, nephew and disciple of the Stoic Chrysippus, placing it in the 2nd century BC.
content.caveat: Only the compact inner core survives — about 8 cm of an original 19–24 cm height — after earlier attempts to open the scroll by hand destroyed its outer layers, so only the lower parts of the columns remain.

licenseScope.EduceLab Data License: the 3.24 µm and 7.91 µm DLS scans

scrolls.desc:
PHerc. 172 is a rolled Herculaneum papyrus from the [Bodleian Library](https://www.bodleian.ox.ac.uk/home) in Oxford, carbonized during the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. It was scanned at the [Diamond Light Source](https://www.diamond.ac.uk) in Oxfordshire, UK.

It is similar in size to PHerc. Paris 4 (Scroll 1).
scrolls.label: PHerc. 172
scrolls.repository: Bodleian Library, Oxford
scrolls.note: About 70% geometrically unrolled as of November 2025.

content.summary: Title recovered — Philodemus, On Vices, Book 1.

readings.status: ~70% geometrically unrolled (Nov 2025); title recovered from the unrolled surface (7.9µm).

images.cap: Ink detection across five consecutive windings (71–75) of the unrolled surface (7.9µm)
Ink detection on a single unrolled winding (w087, 7.9µm)

scrolls.label: PHerc. 139
scrolls.repository: Biblioteca Nazionale di Napoli ‘Vittorio Emanuele III’ (Officina dei Papiri Ercolanesi)
scrolls.note: Title and author recovered — On Gods, Book 8, by the Epicurean philosopher Philodemus. About 20% virtually unrolled.

content.summary: The scroll's title and author were recovered, identifying it as a work by the Epicurean philosopher Philodemus, specifically On Gods, Book 8. Reading the title of a still-rolled scroll reveals what it contains before any of its body columns are studied.
content.caveat: About 20% is virtually unrolled; the title and author are secure, but the body text is still being recovered.

scrolls.desc:
PHerc. 332 is a rolled Herculaneum papyrus from the [Biblioteca Nazionale di Napoli](https://www.bnnonline.it/it/121/officina-dei-papiri-ercolanesi), carbonized during the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. It was first scanned at the [Diamond Light Source](https://www.diamond.ac.uk) in Oxfordshire, UK (2023, at 3.24 µm and 7.91 µm), and rescanned at 2.4 µm at the [ESRF](https://www.esrf.fr) in Grenoble, France (2025).

It is the innermost part of a scroll, known as a *midollo* (marrow), a remnant from physical unwrapping of the larger original.
scrolls.label: PHerc. 332
scrolls.repository: Biblioteca Nazionale di Napoli ‘Vittorio Emanuele III’ (Officina dei Papiri Ercolanesi)

scrolls.desc:
PHerc. Paris 1 Fr 39 is a fragment of a Herculaneum papyrus, carbonized during the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. It was scanned at the [Diamond Light Source](https://www.diamond.ac.uk) in Oxfordshire, UK.

Originally held back for automated scoring in the [Kaggle ink detection competition](https://kaggle.com/competitions/vesuvius-challenge-ink-detection/), this fragment has since been released.

See also: [EduceLab-Scrolls paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.02084), [EduceLab 2023 Info Sheet](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1I6JNrR6A9pMdANbn6uAuXbcDNwjk8qZ2/view?usp=sharing).
scrolls.label: PHerc. Paris 1Fr39
scrolls.repository: Institut de France, Paris

scrolls.desc:
PHerc. 1667 Cr 1 Fr 3 is a fragment from the same scroll as PHerc. 1667 (Scroll 4), from the [Biblioteca Nazionale di Napoli](https://www.bnnonline.it/it/121/officina-dei-papiri-ercolanesi). It was partially opened in 1987 via the Oslo method. It was scanned at the [Diamond Light Source](https://www.diamond.ac.uk) in Oxfordshire, UK.

Listed on [Chartes.it](https://www.chartes.it/index.php?r=document/view&id=1691).

See also: [EduceLab-Scrolls paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.02084), [EduceLab 2023 Info Sheet](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1I6JNrR6A9pMdANbn6uAuXbcDNwjk8qZ2/view?usp=sharing).
